# Transcrição: How_to_Use_Value_Rules_to_Solve_Problems

- **Duração:** 06:47

## [00:00 - 03:07]

This is the pubcast shot. I'm John Loomer. Value rules are one of my favorite features. Let's go. When Meta introduced value rules in 2025, it honestly was a confusing surprise. On one hand, Meta is consistently chipping away at our control. Not only do we have less control now over things like targeting than we had in the past, Meta repeatedly discourages us from doing things that would take back that control. So don't restrict targeting by age or gender, or you'll turn off Advantage Plus. Don't remove an underperforming placement because you'll get better results using Advantage Plus placements. So on one hand, Meta not only launching but encouraging value rules is a surprise. But the more I think about it, the more it all makes sense in this environment. So let me explain. Value rules allow you to bid more or less on specific criteria, like age, gender, location, placement, mobile operating system, and conversion location. Otherwise, you're a bit at the mercy of the algorithm. You would make these adjustments because you have information that Meta doesn't. And that's important here. Let's say you're running a Leads campaign optimized for a lead. It doesn't matter whether it's a website or instant form. Meta's focus is on getting you the most leads within your budget. Cheaper is better. Of course, we know that cheap leads aren't necessarily good leads. And in most cases, advertisers would be willing to spend more to get good leads. When optimizing this way, it's never Meta's consideration. You could end up with tons of cheap, low quality leads. And as far as Meta knows, that's what you want. Let me give you an example. Now, this was something I noticed more than a year ago when running ads for lead generation. I was getting a ton of cheap leads, but they were low quality. I performed a breakdown by age and discovered a very high percentage of those leads came from people who are 65 and up. The next largest group was 55 to 64. Combined, this was making up 70% of my budget and resulting in very cheap, but low quality results. Now, in the past, my response was to restrict targeting by age to eliminate these two groups entirely. Now, that wasn't preferred, of course, because I do have some paying customers in those age groups, especially people in the 55 to 64 group. But if I only eliminated 65 and up, Meta would move all of that budget to 55 to 64. So I'd have to remove that group too. But now, I can address this with value rules. I bid 50% less on people 65 and up and 20% less on people

## [03:07 - 06:08]

aged 55 to 64. The result is that I don't have to remove those groups and Meta spends a far more realistic percentage on them. The 65 and up group now reflects the lowest percentage of my budget with 55 to 64 coming after the 18 to 24 group. It all makes much more sense. So that was an example of using value rules to solve this problem without restricting my targeting entirely. Another example could be related to gender. While this isn't relevant to me, you may have a situation where you serve primarily women-owned businesses. If you optimize for a purchase, Meta will probably spend very little of your budget, if anything, on men. But if you optimize for clicks or engagement or video views, all of that is going to change. Meta only cares about getting you those cheap actions. And if you can get cheap actions from men, it doesn't matter to Meta who buys from you or whether you used audience suggestions. Meta will show your ads to men to get you more results. Now, in the past, you probably would have restricted targeting to remove men entirely to combat this. But with value rules, you can lower your bid by 90% on men or it could be 50% or something so that you don't need to restrict your targeting. A final example is placements. Audience network, yeah, deserved reputation for generating very low quality traffic. It could be accidental clicks, bots, or even click fraud before it's detected. Now, this is rarely an issue if you optimize for a purchase or even a lead. If that low quality traffic doesn't result in the conversion, Meta won't lean into it. But that changes if your performance goal suggests you want link clicks, landing page views, or even traffic adjacent website conversions, you don't care whether you get a purchase. So those traffic adjacent website conversions could be things like view content, standard events, or even custom events related to things like scroll depth and time spent. Even audience network rewarded video placement can be a problem for through play views optimization. Now in the past, I just remove audience network entirely whenever using one of these affected optimizations. But thanks to value rules, I can simply lower my bid by 90% on audience network without removing it entirely. So here's the bottom of the glass. These are just a few examples of how I use value rules to solve problems related to information that I have and Meta doesn't. But there are many more possibilities and Meta will surely add more options since this is a new tool. Prioritize using value rules instead of restricting your targeting or placements. It could also be related to things like conversion location, country, mobile operating system, and more. Value rules is one of my five favorite Meta ads features that I can't live without.
